one_line_takeaway: By backtracking GNN explanations from a coarsened control-flow graph down through a full assembly-instruction-level graph (AFG), the paper achieves genuinely instruction-granular, non-ambiguous malware-detection explanations (beta=1 at basic-block level vs. beta=0 at instruction level in every configuration tested) while showing that mild-to-moderate coarsening (e.g., Kron r=0.25) can simultaneously improve accuracy (+0.4 to +2.2 pts) and explanation quality (lambda score 0.713) over an uncoarsened baseline.
key_finding: Coarsening can improve both inference and explainability at specific, non-maximal settings but degrades both at maximal coarsening. Best average accuracy (C-CFG + B-AFG combined) is 89.8% (+0.4 pts over baseline), achieved by both Kron at r=0.25 and Variation Edges at r=0.75; Kron is preferred since it reaches this accuracy at a higher (more efficient) coarsening level. Best individual C-CFG-level accuracy is 92.3% (Variation Edges, r=0.75, +2.2 pts over baseline); best B-AFG-level accuracy is 90.1% (both Kron r=0.25 and Variation Edges r=0.75, +1.4 pts over baseline). The benign class consistently underperforms the malicious class in inference (roughly 2-3 points lower F1), attributed to the malicious:benign sample ratio of about 1.2:1. For explainability, Kron at r=0.25 achieves the best lambda score of 0.713 (vs. baseline lambda of ~0.637 read from Table III), which the authors attribute mainly to a large drop in Fidelity+ from 0.675 (C-CFG) to 0.030 (B-AFG); Fidelity- changes only slightly (0.083 at C-CFG to 0.042 at B-AFG for that setting), indicating explanations remain highly sufficient at both levels while necessity is concentrated at the coarser level. Classwise, benign samples are markedly more explainable than malicious samples: a baseline lambda gap of 31.2 points between classes. The beta indicator score is maximal (1) for every (C-)CFG-level explanation and minimal (0) for every B-AFG-level explanation across all methods/settings in Table III, which the authors present as direct empirical evidence that CFG/basic-block-level explanations are inherently ambiguous about which instructions matter, while their B-AFG explanations are unambiguous at the instruction level -- framed as the paper's central validation of its granularity claim.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Assembly Flow Graph (AFG), a new program-graph representation that substitutes each control-flow-graph (CFG) basic-block node with its constituent non-branching assembly instructions represented as a linked list, so that GNN-based malware detectors can be explained at the individual instruction level rather than only at the coarse basic-block level. Because full AFGs are too large to coarsen or train on directly, the paper introduces Meta-Coarsening: coarsen the CFG (not the AFG) into a C-CFG using existing graph-coarsening methods, train/explain a GNN on the C-CFG, then backtrack the resulting C-CFG explanation subgraph through the coarsening mapping and through the CFG-to-AFG substitution to obtain a Backtracked AFG (B-AFG) subgraph, on which a second, separate GNN is trained and explained to produce the final instruction-level explanation. The authors also define new evaluation metrics for this two-level explanation setting: a level-aware lambda score (difference between (C-)CFG and B-AFG characterization scores) and a beta indicator score that empirically measures whether an explanation can attribute importance down to individual instructions (binary vs. non-binary explanation vectors).
threat_model: Defensive/detection setting: Windows PE x86 binaries (benign vs. malicious) are classified by GNNs operating on program-flow graphs (CFG/AFG) built via dynamic analysis (angr symbolic execution + capstone instruction decoding). The paper's stated goal is to give malware analysts granular, instruction-level, non-spurious explanations of GNN predictions to increase trust and support manual malware characterization; it explicitly does not build a detector meant to withstand evasion. In the Ethical Considerations section the authors note an adversary could in principle use the produced explanations to identify and remove/perturb the highly characterized malicious subgraph to evade detection, but they do not implement, measure, or evaluate this attack; adversarial evasion, zero-days, and metamorphic code are explicitly listed as out of scope/limitations.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: unclear generalization to binary/executable types beyond Windows PE x86 due to lack of additional labeled datasets; some real-world settings may involve graphs far larger than tested, though the r/epsilon hyperparameters are offered as a mitigation framework rather than a proven solution at scale; the coarsening approaches used (Kron, Variation Edges) are largely deterministic and likely much more computationally expensive than state-of-the-art neural coarsening methods, which the authors say are not available as easily implementable libraries; the full pipeline depends on a dynamic-analysis generation phase (angr) that can incur very large upfront cost in practice, with static CFG/FCG generation suggested as future work; adversarial evasion techniques, zero-day malware, and metamorphic code are explicitly not considered. The paper also notes (without resolving) that malicious-class explanations are consistently less characterizable than benign-class explanations and that further research is needed to understand why.

datasets_used: CIC-DGG-2025 (Dynamically Generated Graphs for Malware Analysis; Shokouhinejad et al. 2025)
DikeDataset (source of benign samples within CIC-DGG-2025)
PE Malware Machine Learning Dataset / PMMLD (source of malicious samples within CIC-DGG-2025)
quantitative_results: Best average accuracy (C-CFG+B-AFG combined): 89.8%, +0.4 pts over baseline, via Kron r=0.25 and Variation Edges r=0.75
Best C-CFG-level accuracy: 92.3% via Variation Edges r=0.75 (+2.2 pts over baseline)
Best B-AFG-level accuracy: 90.1% via Kron r=0.25 and Variation Edges r=0.75 (+1.4 pts over baseline)
B-AFG accuracy range across settings: 85.2%-90.1%; C-CFG accuracy range: 81.0%-92.3%
F1 scores generally 85-90% across most configurations (Table II)
Benign class underperforms malicious class in F1 by roughly 2-3 points across settings
Fidelity+ drop driving best-lambda case: 0.675 (C-CFG) to 0.030 (B-AFG)
Fidelity- change in same case: 0.083 (C-CFG) to 0.042 (B-AFG)
Classwise baseline lambda gap between benign and malicious: 31.2 points
Beta indicator score: 1 (maximal) for all (C-)CFG-level explanations, 0 (minimal) for all B-AFG-level explanations, across every method/r combination in Table III
Instruction encoding dimensionality: 1076-dim feature vector + 27 out-of-vocabulary bits = 1103 bits total; B-AFG node embedding uses a reduced 201-dim opcode-only feature
18 GCN models trained total (3 layers, 50% dropout, hidden dim 128, output dim 2, Adam lr=0.001, 150 epochs, batch size 8)
Train/test split: 80/20, stratified classwise (45% benign / 55% malicious), not stratified by source dataset
Selection policy epsilon fixed at 0.10 (10% of nodes) using Top Edge Selection (TES) for all experiments
baselines_compared: Kron reduction coarsening (Shuman et al. Laplacian pyramid / Loukas library) at r in {0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.999}
Variation Edges coarsening (Loukas, local variation edge method) at r in {0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.999}

Explainer comparison (appendix only, not the paper's main results): Integrated Gradients vs. Guided Backpropagation vs. Saliency (all via Captum); GNNExplainer and PGExplainer discussed but excluded from main experiments based on prior work's (ref [10], same author group) finding of lower consistency for this setting
